title: Synthesis of Nanometer-Sized SiC Whiskers in the Arc-Discharge
abstract:
teresis loop compared to that observed for particles in pow-
der form.
In this communication, synthesis of 10.7 nm ferrite nano-
crystals doped with 3.5 % in mass of cobalt ions is described.
Organization of nanoparticles is controlled by the method
of deposition on a substrate. In an applied magnetic field,
deposition leads to formation of well-aligned nanoparticles
on a very large scale forming a flat surface. In the absence of
an applied field, the particles self-arrange on the substrate
by forming large spheres with a large coverage. Magnetic
properties of nanocrystals differing by their organizations
are presented: when particles are aligned, the hysteresis
loop is straightened and the magnetic parameters such as
remanence and coercivity notably increase. These changes
in the magnetic properties are related to the demagnetizing
factor and to the orientation of the magnetic moments of
the particles along one of the easy magnetization axes.
Received: August 31, 1999
Final version: November 8, 1999

Synthesis of Nanometer-Sized SiC Whiskers in
the Arc-Discharge**
By Torsten Seeger*, Philipp Kohler-Redlich, and
Manfred Rühle
The interest in nanostructured materials stems from the
dramatic changes in physical properties of the solid state at
small dimensions.[1] One of the most spectacular discoveries
in this field was the photoluminescence of Si as a result of
quantum confinement.[2±4] In addition, blue-light emitting
SiC nanoparticles were discovered recently.[5] SiC is partic-
ularly attractive due to its excellent mechanical properties,
high chemical resistivity, and its potential application as a
functional ceramic or a high temperature semiconductor.[6,7]
Nanometer-sized SiC particles have been synthesized by la-
ser- or plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition tech-
niques.[8±10] In recent years the synthesis of nanosized SiC
whiskers has received considerable attention. Dai et al. pro-
duced SiC nanowhiskers by chemical vapor reaction of sili-
con halides with carbon nanotube templates.[11] SiO2-
sheathed SiC nanowhiskers were synthesized by Meng et al.
by the carbothermal reduction of mesoporous SiO2,[12] and
by Zhang et al. by laser ablation of a mixed powder contain-
ing the elements B, N, C, Si, O, and Li.[13]
In this communication we report for the first time the
synthesis of nanometer-sized SiC whiskers using the arc-dis-
charge process. This approach has previously only been suc-
cessful for the synthesis of graphite-encapsulated nanopar-
ticles of a large variety of elements and their carbides.[14±18]
The whiskers were characterized by electron microscopy
and a growth mechanism is discussed.
A transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph
of the collaret deposit from the arc-discharge evaporation
of electrodes containing an Fe catalyst is shown in Fig-
ure 1a. Two types of structures are visible: nanosized
Adv. Mater. 2000, 12, No. 4

Adv. Mater. 2000, 12, No. 4
equiaxed particles at the bottom edges of the micrograph
and rod-like structures. The rods are of about 10 nm diam-
eter and more than 100 nm in length; the diameter of the
particles is in the range 10±20 nm. Rods were only found
when the iron catalyst was present in the arc-discharge
process.
Electron diffraction and high resolution transmission elec-
tron microscopy (HRTEM) were performed in order to ob-
tain information about the structure of the nanometer-sized
particles and rods. The small dimensions of the structures do
not allow the examination of a single rod by conventional se-
lected area diffraction (SAD). Figure 1b shows the SAD
pattern of an area containing some rods and particles and
Figure 1c shows the corresponding radial averaged intensity
profile. Peaks corresponding to spacings of 0.25, 0.16, 0.14,
and 0.13 nm are visible.
In Figure 2a,b HRTEM micrographs of two different
rods with lattice fringes parallel and perpendicular to the
rod axis are shown. The rods are surrounded by an amor-
phous layer. Only rods with a fringe orientation perpendic-
ular or parallel to the rods axis were found. For both orien-
tations the lattice fringe spacing of
0.243 ± 0.010 nm was determined by
using an internal gold reference.
In the electron energy loss spec-
trum
(EELS)
of
Figure 3a
taken
from an individual rod, the Si-L, C-K,
and Si-K edges can be identified. No
traces of iron, oxygen, or nitrogen
could be detected.
The chemical composition was de-
rived from quantification of the Si-L
and
C-K
edges
using
hydrogenic
cross-sections.[19] The carbon content
of the nanorods was determined to
be (63 ± 4) at.-%. The carbon content
of a SiC reference was found to be
(49 ± 3) at.-% using the same proce-
dure. The carbon excess for the nano-
rods results from the amorphous car-
bon surrounding (compare Fig. 2).
Chemical shifts and the energy loss
near edge structure (ELNES) of a
EEL core-loss edge of an element are
characteristic for the bonding state
and its local environment.[20] In Fig-
ure 3b the ELNES of the Si-L edge
for a rod and a particle are compared
to the Si and SiC references. The simi-
larity of the edges of the rod and the
particle to the edge of the SiC ref-
erence is obvious. Also the energy val-
ue of the edge onset of the Si-L edges
corresponds to that reported for SiC in
the literature.[21,22] However, a broad-
ening of the main spectral features is
visible in the near edge structure for the nanorods. Similarly
the Si-K edge (Fig. 3c) measured for a rod matches better to
that of the SiC reference, also revealing a broadening.
The most prominent peak of the SAD pattern corre-
sponds to the {111} (dhkl = 0.252 nm) spacing of cubic
b-SiC. The nanorods consist of b-SiC, which is also consis-
tent with the HRTEM lattice fringe spacing (Fig. 2).
The orientation of the lattice fringes parallel or perpendi-
cular to the whisker axis suggests a single-crystalline whisker
growth of b-SiC with a preferred <111> growth direction.
Both chemical composition and ELNES clearly show
that the nanorods consist of a SiC phase. The broadening
of the Si EEL spectra can either be an effect of reduced
crystallographic order of the observed nanorods (personal
communication with Dr. G. Duscher) or/and a solid solu-
tion of Fe below the detection limit in the SiC lattice, both
changing the binding energy of the core level electrons and
the energy of the unoccupied states in the Si.
Catalytic growth of the reported SiC whiskers can be de-
duced from the fact that they were only observed when Fe
was present in the reaction chamber.
Fig. 1. a) TEM micrograph of the collaret deposit formed by arc-discharge evaporation of a Si/Fe-filled
graphite electrode. Whiskers of about 10 nm in diameter and more than 100 nm in length are visible. Many
small particles can be seen at the bottom edges of the micrograph. The background structure is the amor-
phous carbon film of the TEM grid. b) SAD pattern of an area containing some nanometer-sized whiskers
and particles. c) The radially averaged intensity profile of the pattern drawn as a function of the real lattice
fringe spacing, dhkl. The peaks correspond to fringe spacings of 0.25, 0.16, 0.14, and 0.13 nm.
